q: What is a token, and why does token count matter in practice?
a: A token is the atomic subword unit an LLM processes, roughly 3-4 characters or about 0.75 words of English, produced by BPE tokenization rather than word-splitting. Token count drives cost (APIs charge per input and output token), latency (more tokens mean longer generation), and whether you fit inside the context window. Code, JSON, punctuation, and non-English text tokenize less efficiently, so trim unnecessary context and keep system prompts tight. Tokenization is also model-specific, so the same string can count differently across providers.
fj: A token is basically a word.
fs: A token is a subword unit; 1 token ≈ 0.75 words for English prose, less for code or rare words.

followups.q: How do you count tokens before sending an API request?
followups.a: Use the provider's tokenizer library — tiktoken for OpenAI, or the Anthropic token-count endpoint. Both let you count tokens locally or via API before you send, so you can guard against context overflow and estimate cost.

followups.q: What happens if you exceed the context window?
followups.a: The API returns an error (or silently truncates, depending on provider and config). You handle it by chunking the input, summarizing earlier conversation, or using a model with a larger window — not by just hoping it works.

q: What is temperature, and how do you set it for different tasks?
a: Temperature scales the logits before sampling: near 0 makes the model deterministic and pick the top-probability token, while higher values flatten the distribution and add randomness. Use temperature 0 for classification, extraction, and factual Q&A where you want the same correct answer every time, and 0.7-1.0 for creative writing or brainstorming where you want variety. Top-p is complementary, not interchangeable: it clips the candidate set to the smallest group whose cumulative probability exceeds p, so a common production default is temperature 0.7 with top_p 0.95. Avoid combining both extremes, which either collapses all variety or produces incoherence.
fj: I just leave it at whatever the default is.
fs: I set temperature by task type: 0 for factual/structured output, 0.7+ for creative generation.

followups.q: What's the downside of temperature 0 for creative tasks?
followups.a: The model will give the exact same output every run, which is great for evals but bad for variety. If you're generating marketing copy or brainstorming ideas you want 10 different options — temperature 0 gives you the same one repeated.

followups.q: How do you test the effect of temperature changes?
followups.a: Run the same prompt N times at each temperature setting and compare outputs — variance in output, factual accuracy, and format. Track this in an eval harness so temperature decisions are data-driven, not vibes.

q: What is an embedding, and what can you use it for in an LLM application?
a: An embedding is a dense fixed-size vector that represents the meaning of a text, so semantically similar texts sit close together in the embedding space. It is produced by a separate embedding model (not the generation LLM), which outputs vectors rather than text and is typically smaller, cheaper, and faster. Embeddings power semantic search, RAG retrieval, clustering, classification, and deduplication, with similarity usually measured by cosine similarity or dot product. In RAG the embedding model is the retrieval backbone, so upgrading it often improves accuracy more than swapping the generation model.
fj: An embedding is what the LLM outputs.
fs: An embedding is a vector from a separate embedding model; the LLM generates text — different models, different calls.

followups.q: Why cosine similarity instead of Euclidean distance?
followups.a: Cosine similarity measures the angle between vectors, ignoring magnitude, which is what you want for semantic meaning. A long document and a short document about the same topic can have different magnitude vectors but similar directions — cosine captures that; Euclidean doesn't.

followups.q: How do you pick an embedding model?
followups.a: Check the MTEB leaderboard for your task type (retrieval, classification, etc.), consider dimension size (higher = more expressive but more memory/latency), and match the model to your domain if available. Smaller models like text-embedding-3-small are often sufficient and much cheaper.

q: What is a system prompt, and how do you use it well?
a: The system prompt is a privileged instruction block sent before the user turn that sets the model's role, persona, output format, and constraints for the entire conversation. Treat it as a versioned, tested specification rather than a throwaway sentence, keeping it focused and explicit and putting the most important constraints first since models attend more reliably to early instructions. It is behavioral guidance, not a security boundary: a determined user can prompt the model to ignore or reveal it via jailbreaks. For dynamic context, move it out of the system prompt into a retrieval step and use prompt caching for the static portion.
fj: System prompt is like extra instructions before the user message.
fs: System prompt is the spec for the model's behavior — role, format, constraints, versioned and tested like code.

followups.q: How do you handle a system prompt that's getting very long?
followups.a: First, trim redundant instructions. Then use prompt caching — with Anthropic, mark the static system prompt block with cache_control so it's cached after the first call. For dynamic context, move it out of the system prompt and into a retrieval step (RAG). A bloated system prompt hurts latency and cost.

followups.q: What's the difference between a system prompt and a user prompt?
followups.a: System prompt is author-controlled — it sets the frame for the whole session and the user usually never sees it. User prompt is what the end user sends per turn. The model weights them differently; system instructions generally override conflicting user instructions, but not reliably for adversarial prompts.

q: When would you use streaming responses vs waiting for the full response from an LLM?
a: Streaming sends tokens as they are generated, improving perceived latency for chat UIs and long-form generation without changing total generation time or compute. Batch (non-streaming) waits for the complete response, which is simpler to implement and better for backend pipelines, classification, and extraction where you need the full output before acting and want easier retries. For large offline workloads with no UX requirement, provider async batch APIs like OpenAI Batch and Anthropic Message Batches offer roughly 50% cost discounts at up to 24h turnaround. Error handling mid-stream is genuinely harder since you must buffer partial output and detect error events in the SSE stream.
fj: Streaming is faster than waiting for the full response.
fs: Streaming doesn't change generation speed — it improves perceived latency for the user by showing tokens as they arrive.

followups.q: How do you handle errors mid-stream?
followups.a: You need to handle partial output — buffer tokens, detect error events in the SSE stream, and decide whether to show partial content or clear it. Batch is easier: you just check the full response status. Streaming error handling is genuinely more complex.

followups.q: When would async batch APIs save the most money?
followups.a: High-volume classification, bulk extraction, or nightly enrichment jobs where the result doesn't need to be real-time. You submit thousands of requests, pay 50% less per token, and get results back within 24h. The savings are real at scale.

q: How do you get reliable structured output (e.g. JSON) from an LLM in production?
a: Use the provider's structured-output or tool-calling mode with a strict schema so the decoder cannot emit invalid structure, rather than asking for JSON in the prompt. Keep the schema flat and typed, then validate on the way out and retry on parse failure by feeding the validation error back so the model self-repairs. Add idempotency, bounded retries, and a fallback path so one bad generation does not break the pipeline. Treat parse-failure rate and per-field accuracy as real SLOs you measure and alert on, since at high volume even a small failure rate corrupts many records.
fj: I ask it to return JSON and parse it.
fs: I use structured-output/tool calling with a schema, then validate and retry on failure.

followups.q: Why not just regex/repair the output?
followups.a: Repair is a brittle band-aid that hides real failures and silently corrupts data. Constrain generation up front and validate; repair only as a last-resort fallback you alert on, not the happy path.

followups.q: How does prompt caching interact with structured output?
followups.a: Cache the static system prompt and schema definition prefix. On Anthropic, mark those blocks with cache_control so they're cached at 0.1x the input token cost. The dynamic user input comes after the cache breakpoint, so you still pay full price for it — but the schema preamble is nearly free on repeat calls.

followups.q: What do you do when the schema changes in production?
followups.a: Version the schema alongside the prompt in git. Deploy schema changes with a prompt version bump and run your structured-output eval suite against the new version before cutover. Treat schema changes as breaking changes — downstream consumers depend on the field names.

q: Explain prompt caching: how it works, when it pays off, and how you structure prompts to maximize cache hits.
a: Prompt caching reuses the transformer's KV attention states from an identical prompt prefix, skipping recomputation for the cached tokens, so it requires exact prefix matching and any change invalidates everything after it. Anthropic uses explicit cache_control markers with reads at 0.1x base input cost and writes at 1.25x, plus a default 5-minute TTL, while OpenAI does it automatically with 50-90% discounts on cached prefixes of at least 1,024 tokens. It pays off for high-volume repeated calls sharing a large static system prompt, tool definitions, or document context. Structure prompts static-first (tools, then system instructions, then fixed context) with variable user input last, and note this is exact-prefix KV reuse, not application-layer semantic response caching.
fj: Prompt caching is like response caching — if the question is similar you get the same answer.
fs: Prompt caching reuses KV states for identical prefixes — it's inside the model, not application-layer semantic caching.

followups.q: What's the difference between prompt caching and semantic caching?
followups.a: Prompt caching reuses KV attention states for identical prompt prefixes — it's inside the model forward pass. Semantic caching caches the full response for semantically similar queries (e.g. in a Redis layer) — it skips the API call entirely. Semantic caching is coarser and application-layer; prompt caching is provider-side and exact-match.

followups.q: How do you warm the cache before user traffic arrives?
followups.a: With Anthropic, fire a dummy request with max_tokens=0 targeting the static prefix at startup. Check cache_creation_input_tokens in the response to confirm the write happened. This ensures the first real user request gets a cache hit instead of a cold miss.

followups.q: When does prompt caching actually hurt you?
followups.a: When the 'static' prefix changes every call (e.g. includes a timestamp or session ID) — you get cache writes but zero hits, paying the 25% write premium for nothing. Also when the prompt is below the minimum token threshold (1,024 for most models), caching simply doesn't activate.

q: How do you reduce hallucination in a production LLM feature?
a: Hallucination is a property of probabilistic generation that you reduce in layers rather than eliminate. The biggest lever is grounding the model in retrieved facts via RAG and instructing it to answer only from that context, combined with temperature 0 for factual tasks and explicit abstention prompting so it says when it does not know. Use structured output with typed fields, which confabulates less than free prose, and add a validation layer using NLI or LLM-as-judge faithfulness checks to flag claims not supported by the retrieved context. Then track hallucination rate as a measurable SLO with monitoring and alerting rather than treating it as just a prompt-quality issue.
fj: Use a better/bigger model.
fs: Ground the model in retrieved context, set temperature 0, prompt for abstention, and validate faithfulness — layered mitigation, not model magic.

followups.q: How do you run a faithfulness check at scale?
followups.a: Use an LLM-as-judge prompt that scores each answer against the retrieved context (Likert scale or binary faithful/not). Run this asynchronously on a sample in prod and on 100% of your eval set. Track faithfulness rate per feature and alert on drops.

followups.q: Doesn't RAG hallucination happen too — the model ignores the retrieved context?
followups.a: Yes — a model can retrieve the right passage and still ignore it. The fix is a stronger prompt ('answer ONLY using the context below'), few-shot examples of correct grounding, and checking the model's temperature. A faithfulness check catches this: if the claim isn't in the retrieved text, it's a hallucination regardless of whether RAG was used.

q: How do you optimize LLM cost and latency without sacrificing output quality?
a: The highest-ROI lever is model routing: benchmark quality by task category and route each to the cheapest model that hits the SLO, sending only complex tasks to a frontier model. Layer on prompt caching for static system prompts and tool definitions, prompt compression to cut redundant input tokens, and async batch APIs for latency-tolerant offline workloads at roughly 50% off. Streaming improves perceived latency in chat UIs at no extra cost but does not reduce spend. Never optimize blind: measure token spend per feature, quality per model tier, and latency p50/p95 before and after each change.
fj: Just use a cheaper model everywhere.
fs: I benchmark quality by task category, route each category to the cheapest model that hits the SLO, and measure before shipping.

followups.q: How do you decide the routing threshold between models?
followups.a: Run the same eval set on both the small and large model. Find the task categories where quality drops below your SLO on the small model — those stay on the large model. Everything else routes to the small model. Track routing distribution in prod and alert on quality regression.

followups.q: What's TTFTT and why does it matter for streaming?
followups.a: Time-to-first-token: how long before the user sees the first character. In streaming chat UIs this is the dominant latency metric — users perceive a fast TTFTT as 'snappy' even if total generation is long. Prompt compression, caching, and smaller models all reduce TTFTT.

q: How do you decide which LLM provider and model to use for a new feature?
a: Start from the task (reasoning type, output format, context length) and run your own eval set rather than trusting general benchmarks or hype, since the best model is task-specific and changes every few months. Establish a quality ceiling with a frontier model, then find the cheapest tier that still clears your quality SLO. Factor in provider constraints that often narrow the choice before technical evaluation: latency SLAs, data residency, compliance like HIPAA and SOC2, pricing, and rate limits. Keep the selection process repeatable so the same eval harness picks today's model and re-evaluates the next release.
fj: We use GPT-4o because it's the best.
fs: I run the task through our eval set across candidate models — 'best' is task-specific and changes with every release.

followups.q: How quickly should you switch to a new model when a better one releases?
followups.a: Run it through your eval set. If it beats the current model on quality and the migration is low-risk (same API shape, same pricing tier), switch. If quality is marginal or the pricing jumps, stay put. Never switch based on benchmarks alone.

q: What is function/tool calling in LLM APIs, and when would you use it over plain prompt output?
a: Tool calling lets you declare functions with JSON Schema, and the model outputs a structured tool-call object (name plus typed arguments) when it decides to invoke one; critically, the model does not execute the function, your code does and optionally returns results to continue. Because it constrains the sampler to a valid schema, it is more reliable than prompt-based JSON, so use it for trustworthy structured extraction, agents that take real actions, and type-safe argument parsing. Plain prompt output is fine for free-form generation or classification where a schema would over-constrain. Versus structured output mode, tool calling is the pattern where the model decides which action to take, while structured output always returns one fixed shape.
fj: The model calls the function directly.
fs: The model outputs a structured tool-call object; my code executes the function and optionally returns the result.

lines: # model picks tool; YOU run it

followups.q: What's the difference between tool calling and structured output mode?
followups.a: Tool calling declares available functions and the model decides if/which to invoke, returning a tool-call message. Structured output constrains the entire response to a schema you define — the model always returns the schema shape. Tool calling is for agent action-taking where the model decides what to do; structured output is for extraction where you always want the same shape.

followups.q: How do you handle a model that calls the wrong tool?
followups.a: Clear tool descriptions with explicit examples of when to use each, few-shot demonstrations, and restrict the tool set to what's actually needed for the task. In agents, validate the tool name before executing and return a structured error if an unknown or inapplicable tool is called.

q: Fine-tuning vs RAG vs prompt engineering — how do you decide which to use, and when do you combine them?
a: Start with prompt engineering since it is cheapest and fastest and solves more than expected with few-shot and careful instructions. Use RAG when the gap is knowledge or freshness, injecting current, citable facts the model lacks, and fine-tuning when the gap is behavior, format, or style rather than facts, since baking knowledge into weights goes stale and is hard to audit. The diagnostic is whether giving the context in the prompt would fix it (a retrieval problem) versus needing consistent behavior (a fine-tuning problem). They compose: fine-tune for format and tone plus RAG for knowledge is the production hybrid, though a fine-tuned model's confidence can override retrieved context, which you manage with grounding prompts and faithfulness evals.
fj: Fine-tune it on our docs so it knows them.
fs: Docs are knowledge — that's RAG. Fine-tuning is for behavior/format, and knowledge baked in weights goes stale.

followups.q: When is fine-tuning clearly the right call over RAG?
followups.a: Stable, narrow task with abundant labeled examples where you need consistent format/tone or want to shrink a large prompted model into a smaller, cheaper one. High-volume classification, extraction with a fixed schema, or brand-voice generation are the canonical cases. The knowledge doesn't change, so staleness isn't a risk.

followups.q: Can RAG and fine-tuning conflict?
followups.a: Yes — a model fine-tuned to be confident about domain topics can override retrieved context that contradicts its weights. You manage it with a strong grounding prompt ('answer only from the context below'), faithfulness evals, and optionally a lower fine-tuning learning rate so the model doesn't over-fit to confidence.

followups.q: What's the 2025 best practice for fine-tuning efficiently?
followups.a: LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) or QLoRA for memory-constrained setups — train adapter layers on a frozen base model, requiring a fraction of the memory of full fine-tuning. DPO (Direct Preference Optimization) for alignment from preference data without a separate reward model. Both are now available in open frameworks (HuggingFace TRL, LLaMA-Factory).

q: How do you design an eval harness for an LLM feature in production?
a: First define what correct means with a curated test set of 100-500 representative inputs and expected outputs, prioritizing quality and edge cases over quantity. Layer evals into unit checks on individual steps (retrieval recall, parse-failure rate), end-to-end task success, and regression tests for known failure modes, and run them in CI as a blocking gate on every prompt, model, or schema change. Calibrate any LLM-as-judge against human raters to above 85% agreement before trusting it at scale. Separately monitor sampled live outputs asynchronously and alert on quality drops, since CI evals catch regressions while prod monitoring catches distribution and model drift.
fj: We manually check outputs after each change.
fs: Evals run in CI on every prompt/model change — manual review is for calibration, not regression detection.

followups.q: How do you handle eval for non-deterministic tasks like creative writing?
followups.a: Define a rubric (tone, relevance, length, format) and score with LLM-as-judge. Run multiple samples and aggregate to reduce variance. Calibrate the judge on human-rated examples. You can't require exact string match, but you can define measurable quality dimensions and score them reliably.

followups.q: What's the minimum eval set size to be meaningful?
followups.a: 100–200 examples is typically enough to detect regressions at the 5% level with statistical significance. The quality of examples matters more than the quantity — 50 carefully curated, edge-case-rich examples often detect more regressions than 500 randomly sampled ones.

followups.q: How do you handle model version changes from the provider?
followups.a: Treat them as breaking changes: pin to a specific model version in prod, run the full eval suite on the new version before upgrading, and plan a rollout window. Providers deprecate model versions on schedule — staying on an old version is a known tech debt you track and pay down deliberately.

q: How do you build production reliability and guardrails for an LLM feature that handles user input?
a: Think of it as middleware: input guardrails first (a fast classifier or rules detecting off-topic input, PII, jailbreaks, and prompt injection), then the model call, then output guardrails validating schema, content policy, and citation faithfulness before serving. Defend against prompt injection by treating user input as untrusted data and keeping it structurally separate from system instructions, never concatenating raw user text into the system prompt, and sanitize retrieved context too. Add circuit breakers and fallbacks so an unavailable or slow model serves a cached or degraded response instead of failing open. Wrap everything in observability: log requests and responses with PII scrubbing, trace per-step latency, and alert on error rate, quality score, and cost.
fj: Just add a system prompt saying 'be safe and helpful.'
fs: Guardrails are middleware — input classifier, output validator, fallbacks, prompt injection isolation, and observability on every layer.

followups.q: How do you handle a model that starts returning policy violations in prod?
followups.a: You need observability first — you see the violation in your output-validation metrics. Immediate: add an output filter to catch and reject the pattern. Short-term: identify if it's prompt drift (the prompt changed), model drift (provider updated the model), or data drift (users found a new jailbreak). Long-term: add the pattern to your eval set so it's a regression test going forward.

followups.q: How do you defend against indirect prompt injection in RAG?
followups.a: Retrieved documents can contain adversarial instructions meant to override the system prompt. Defenses: sanitize retrieved text (strip HTML, escape special chars), use a structured prompt format that clearly delineates retrieved context from instructions, and run a classifier on retrieved chunks for injection patterns. Full defense is hard — you monitor and add patterns as you see them.

q: Self-host an open-source LLM vs use a cloud API — how do you make the decision?
a: Cloud APIs give zero MLOps overhead, frontier capability, and per-token pricing, making them the right call for most products especially early-stage and below the cost crossover of roughly hundreds of millions of tokens per month. Self-hosting open models keeps data inside your boundary (often the decisive factor for HIPAA, SOC2, or GDPR workloads), gives predictable cost at high throughput and customization freedom, but requires GPUs, a serving stack like vLLM or TGI, and ongoing ops capacity. Decide on a matrix of data sensitivity, volume/cost crossover, capability gap, and ops capacity rather than cost alone. A common answer is hybrid: API for frontier and low-volume tasks, self-hosted fine-tuned models for high-volume or PII-sensitive processing.
fj: Self-hosting is always cheaper.
fs: Below a few hundred million tokens/month, API is usually cheaper when you factor in GPU infra and ops overhead.

followups.q: What serving stack would you use for self-hosted inference?
followups.a: vLLM (2024–2025 standard) for its PagedAttention (efficient KV cache management) and continuous batching — dramatically higher throughput than naive inference. TGI (Text Generation Inference) is the alternative. For smaller models on constrained hardware, llama.cpp for CPU/low-VRAM inference. Layer an OpenAI-compatible API adapter on top so applications can switch between self-hosted and cloud API without code changes.

followups.q: How do you quantify the cost crossover point?
followups.a: Estimate monthly token volume (input + output separately, since output is usually more expensive). Compare against API per-token cost. Then estimate self-host cost: GPU hours × utilization × instance cost + engineering overhead + ops time. The crossover for a 70B model on A100 vs GPT-4o API is typically in the 100M–500M tokens/month range, but varies widely by model size, GPU, and utilization.

q: How do you approach a failing LLM feature in production — your quality metrics dropped and users are complaining?
a: Bisect the pipeline before drawing conclusions: log retrieved chunks, the prompt sent, and the model response to isolate whether retrieval, model, prompt, or data is at fault. Check what changed using git blame on prompts and deployment logs, since a provider's silent model-version update can degrade your task even after passing their benchmarks. Roll back first under pressure, reverting a bad prompt or pinning to the prior model version, then characterize which failure categories are degrading by running the failing examples through your eval suite. Fix the root cause, run the full eval suite rather than just the failing cases, and redeploy with monitoring on the affected metrics.
fj: I'd try different prompts until it gets better.
fs: I'd bisect the pipeline, check what changed, roll back if possible, characterize the failure categories, then fix with eval validation.

followups.q: How do you prevent this failure mode from happening again?
followups.a: Add the failing examples to the eval suite. Automate eval in CI so the failure would have been caught before deploy. Set up prod monitoring on the affected quality metrics with alerting. Pin the model version and have a documented upgrade process that includes running the full eval suite on the new version.

followups.q: What if the failure is in retrieval, not the model?
followups.a: Inspect the retrieved chunks: are the right documents being surfaced? Check if the source data changed (index is stale, new document formats broke chunking). Run recall@k on a labeled set to measure retrieval quality directly. Fix the retrieval issue (re-chunk, re-embed, update the index) and validate before re-enabling.

q: How do you decide when to use an LLM vs a traditional ML model or deterministic code for a task?
a: Escalate deliberately: use deterministic code first when logic is expressible as rules, regex, or lookups since it is fast, cheap, testable, and never hallucinates, then traditional ML like a fine-tuned BERT when you have labeled data and a defined input/output schema. Reach for an LLM only when the bottleneck is genuine semantic language understanding (summarization, complex extraction, reasoning over unstructured text, conversation) or the domain changes too fast for a labeled training set. The real question is whether the task needs language understanding or just pattern matching, since LLMs are overkill and expensive for the latter. The practical synthesis is hybrid: an LLM for the fuzzy understanding step feeding deterministic code for business logic.
fj: LLMs are so good now, we use them for everything.
fs: LLMs for language-understanding tasks; deterministic code or traditional ML for pattern-matching and structured prediction — LLMs are overkill and expensive for the latter.

followups.q: Give an example where you'd choose traditional ML over an LLM.
followups.a: Spam classification with 100k labeled emails: fine-tune a DistilBERT, run it locally, 99%+ accuracy, $0 per inference. Using GPT-4o for the same task is 100x more expensive per classification with no accuracy benefit — and you can't explain the decision in a regulated context.

q: Compare fine-tuning, RAG, and prompting: when does each dominate, when do they fail, and how do you validate the choice?
a: Prompting needs zero infra and is surprisingly powerful with few-shot and chain-of-thought, but fails at context-length limits, beyond the knowledge cutoff, or when maintaining consistent format at scale becomes a burden. RAG adds current, citable, swappable knowledge but fails on poor retrieval quality, high retrieval-plus-generation latency, or domains too narrow to index. Fine-tuning fixes behavioral gaps in format and tone and cuts per-call cost at high volume, but fails when used to teach facts (they go stale) or when the training set is unrepresentative of production. Validate by running every viable approach through the same production-representative eval set and picking the one that hits the SLO at the lowest total cost of compute, ops, and iteration velocity.
fj: Fine-tuning is always the best for our use case.
fs: Fine-tuning fixes behavior gaps; RAG fixes knowledge gaps. I validate the choice with an eval set — not intuition.

followups.q: How does LoRA change the fine-tuning decision?
followups.a: LoRA makes fine-tuning much more accessible: you train adapter layers on a frozen base model, requiring 1/10th the VRAM of full fine-tuning, and you can swap adapters at serving time. This lowers the bar for fine-tuning experiments significantly — you can now iterate on fine-tuning almost as fast as on prompting, which changes the economics of the approach.

followups.q: Can you have too much context in prompting?
followups.a: Yes — 'lost in the middle' is a documented phenomenon where models underweight context in the middle of very long prompts. Frontier models have improved, but very long contexts still see quality degradation on the middle sections. This is one reason RAG (retrieving only the relevant chunks) beats stuffing the full document, even when the document fits in the context window.

q: What is quantization, and what do INT8, INT4, and FP8 trade off when serving an LLM?
a: Quantization stores weights (and sometimes activations) in fewer bits than the FP16/BF16 training precision, cutting VRAM and memory bandwidth so the model fits on smaller or fewer GPUs. INT8 roughly halves memory with usually negligible quality loss; INT4 quarters it and is what lets a 70B model fit on a single GPU, but degrades complex reasoning and math more; FP8 sits close to BF16 quality and is increasingly the data-center default because modern GPUs run it natively. Most production setups are weight-only (W4A16): weights in 4-bit, activations left in FP16, since single-stream decode is memory-bandwidth-bound and weight fetches dominate.
fj: Quantization just makes the model smaller and a bit worse.
fs: It cuts memory/bandwidth; weight-only INT4 (W4A16) wins because decode is memory-bound, and FP8 stays near BF16 quality.

followups.q: Why is W4A16 the common choice instead of also quantizing activations?
followups.a: Single-stream decode is memory-bandwidth-bound, so the cost is dominated by fetching weights, not activation math. W4A16 shrinks the weight fetch (the bottleneck) while leaving activations in FP16 to preserve accuracy. Quantizing activations too (W4A4) only helps when you're compute-bound, like large-batch prefill, and it's much harder to keep accurate.

followups.q: Why does INT4 hurt reasoning and math more than other tasks?
followups.a: Multi-step reasoning compounds small per-token errors — a slightly-wrong logit early in a chain derails the whole derivation. INT4's coarser weight representation perturbs the distribution just enough to flip those tipping-point tokens. Factual recall or classification tolerate the noise because one token rarely cascades.

followups.q: Why is FP8 increasingly the data-center default over INT8?
followups.a: Modern GPUs (Hopper, Blackwell) have native FP8 tensor cores, so FP8 runs at full hardware throughput while staying numerically close to BF16. Its floating-point range handles outlier activations better than INT8's fixed scale, so it often skips the calibration headaches INT8 needs, and many models now serve FP8 with effectively no quality loss.

q: GPTQ vs AWQ vs naive round-to-nearest INT4 — why do the calibration-based methods win?
a: Naive round-to-nearest quantizes every weight uniformly and loses accuracy badly at 4-bit because a few outlier weights carry disproportionate signal. GPTQ uses second-order (Hessian) information from a calibration set to quantize weights one column at a time while compensating the error in the remaining weights, preserving accuracy. AWQ instead inspects activation magnitudes on calibration data to find the most salient weight channels and scales them so they survive quantization, leaving the rest aggressive; it is the current best-practice INT4 format for vLLM and avoids GPTQ's per-layer reconstruction cost. Both are post-training and need only a small calibration set, not retraining.
fj: They're all the same, just different libraries for 4-bit.
fs: Naive RTN dies on outliers; GPTQ does Hessian-aware error compensation, AWQ protects activation-salient channels — both calibrated, not blind.

followups.q: What goes wrong if your calibration set doesn't match production traffic?
followups.a: Both `GPTQ` and `AWQ` pick which weights to protect based on activation statistics from the calibration data. If you calibrate on English wiki text but serve code or another language, the salient channels differ and you quantize away weights that actually matter at inference, silently degrading quality on your real distribution. Calibrate on a sample of representative prod inputs.

followups.q: When would you pick GPTQ over AWQ despite AWQ being the vLLM default?
followups.a: `GPTQ`'s per-column Hessian error compensation can edge out `AWQ` on accuracy at very aggressive settings (e.g. 3-bit or 2-bit) where outlier protection alone isn't enough. It's also more mature for some architectures. The tradeoff is `GPTQ`'s slower, more memory-hungry calibration pass versus `AWQ`'s lighter activation-scaling step.

followups.q: Why does picking the wrong few outlier weights wreck 4-bit accuracy so badly?
followups.a: Weight magnitudes in LLMs are heavy-tailed — a tiny fraction of channels carry disproportionate signal. Naive RTN spreads the 4-bit grid uniformly, so those large-magnitude channels get crushed into the same coarse buckets as everything else. Calibrated methods detect and protect them (scale up before quantizing, or compensate the error), which is the whole game at 4-bit.

q: What is the KV cache, why does it dominate memory at long context, and how does GQA help?
a: During autoregressive decoding, each new token must attend to every prior token, so the model caches the key and value tensors of past tokens to avoid recomputing them — turning per-step attention from quadratic to linear. The cache grows linearly with batch size and sequence length, and above roughly 128K context it exceeds the parameter memory itself (e.g. ~135 GB for Llama-70B at 1M tokens in FP16). Grouped-Query Attention (GQA) lets multiple query heads share one set of key/value heads, shrinking the cache by the grouping ratio (commonly 4-8x) with near-equivalent accuracy, which is why it is standard in Llama 3, Mistral, and Gemma. PagedAttention then allocates the cache in fixed blocks like OS virtual memory to cut fragmentation waste and raise throughput.
fj: The KV cache just stores the conversation so far.
fs: It caches past keys/values to make decode linear; past ~128K it outweighs the weights, and GQA shrinks it by sharing KV heads.

followups.q: Why does the KV cache, not the weights, usually cap how many requests you can batch?
followups.a: Weights are loaded once and shared across every request in the batch, so their VRAM footprint is fixed. The KV cache is per-request and grows with sequence length, so as you add concurrent long-context requests it's the cache that exhausts memory first. That's why serving throughput is often KV-cache-bound, and why `PagedAttention` and GQA matter so much.

followups.q: Does GQA hurt model quality, and why is it an acceptable trade?
followups.a: GQA loses a little expressiveness versus full multi-head attention because query heads share KV projections, but the degradation is small and largely recovered by training with GQA from the start (or a short uptraining phase). The 4-8x KV-cache shrink — enabling longer context and bigger batches — far outweighs the marginal accuracy cost, which is why Llama 3, Mistral, and Gemma all ship it.

followups.q: Can you quantize the KV cache, and what breaks?
followups.a: Yes — FP8 or INT8 KV cache roughly halves cache memory and is common in vLLM/TensorRT-LLM. The risk is that keys are sensitive: quantizing them too aggressively distorts attention scores and degrades long-context recall more than weight quantization does. Many setups quantize values harder than keys, or keep the most recent tokens in full precision.

q: How are LLM context windows extended past the pretraining length — what do Position Interpolation, NTK-aware scaling, and YaRN do?
a: Models with RoPE (rotary position embeddings) encode position by rotating query/key vectors at per-dimension frequencies, and they fail to extrapolate to positions longer than seen in pretraining. Position Interpolation rescales position indices to squeeze longer sequences into the trained range, but uniformly compressing all frequencies harms high-frequency (local) detail. NTK-aware scaling fixes this by stretching low frequencies more than high ones so fine-grained local structure is preserved. YaRN refines NTK with piecewise per-band frequency scaling plus an attention-temperature adjustment, reaching state-of-the-art long-context performance after fine-tuning on under ~0.1% of pretraining data, and its dynamic variant can extend context at inference with no fine-tuning.
fj: You just set a bigger max_tokens / context length parameter.
fs: RoPE doesn't extrapolate; you rescale its frequencies — PI uniformly, NTK frequency-aware, YaRN piecewise plus attention-temperature, usually with light fine-tuning.

followups.q: Why does uniform Position Interpolation degrade local/short-range detail?
followups.a: Compressing all position indices into the trained range squeezes the high-frequency `RoPE` dimensions that encode fine, adjacent-token relationships. Those high frequencies were already near their resolution limit, so uniform scaling blurs local structure. NTK-aware scaling fixes this by stretching low frequencies more and leaving high frequencies nearly untouched.

followups.q: Why does YaRN add an attention-temperature term on top of frequency scaling?
followups.a: Stretching the context lengthens attention distributions, which makes the softmax flatter and dilutes how sharply the model focuses. YaRN scales the attention logits (a temperature factor) to recover the original sharpness, so the model attends as decisively at long range as it did at its native length. Frequency rescaling alone fixes position encoding but not this concentration loss.

followups.q: Why does extending context still usually require some fine-tuning?
followups.a: Even with correct frequency rescaling, the model has never seen attention patterns spanning the new length, so its weights aren't adapted to use distant tokens well. A short fine-tune on long sequences (YaRN needs under ~0.1% of pretraining data) teaches the model to actually exploit the extended range. Dynamic/training-free variants exist but typically trade some quality for skipping that step.

q: What is a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) model, and what's the difference between active and total parameters?
a: An MoE replaces the dense feed-forward block with many parallel expert sub-networks plus a router that, per token, picks a small top-k subset to run; only those experts compute, so most of the network is dormant on any given token. This decouples total parameters (all experts, which must be loaded in VRAM) from active parameters (the few actually executed per token, which set the compute and latency). A 400B-total model might activate only ~17B per token, giving near-frontier quality at a fraction of the FLOPs. The 2026 dominant pattern adds a shared expert that always runs alongside the routed ones (DeepSeek, Qwen) to keep stable common knowledge while routed experts specialize.
fj: MoE is several separate models and you pick the best one's answer.
fs: Per token a router fires top-k experts inside one model; total params live in VRAM but only active params cost compute — that's the win.

followups.q: If MoE only activates a few experts, why must you still hold all of them in VRAM?
followups.a: The router picks experts per token, and different tokens in the same sequence (and across a batch) route to different experts, so any expert can be needed at any step. You can't predict the routing ahead of time, so all experts must be resident for low-latency serving. That's why MoE saves compute/FLOPs but not memory — VRAM is sized to total params.

followups.q: What is load balancing in MoE training and why does it matter?
followups.a: Left alone, the router collapses onto a few favorite experts while others starve and never learn — wasted capacity. Training adds an auxiliary load-balancing loss that pushes tokens to spread evenly across experts. At serving time, imbalanced routing also creates stragglers when experts are sharded across GPUs, hurting throughput, so balance matters for both quality and efficiency.

followups.q: Why does the 2026 pattern add an always-on shared expert?
followups.a: Routed experts specialize, but common knowledge (grammar, basic facts) is needed on nearly every token — duplicating it across many routed experts wastes capacity. A shared expert that always runs holds that universal knowledge once, freeing routed experts to specialize. DeepSeek and Qwen use this to get more effective capacity per active parameter and more stable routing.

q: What is knowledge distillation, and how does it differ from quantization and pruning?
a: Distillation trains a smaller student model to mimic a larger teacher by matching the teacher's full output distribution (soft labels / logits), not just the hard correct answer — the relative probabilities of wrong options carry extra signal about how the teacher reasons, so the student learns more than from raw labels alone. It produces a genuinely smaller, cheaper model that you can then serve at full precision. That is different from quantization, which keeps the same architecture but stores it in fewer bits, and from pruning, which removes redundant weights or neurons from an existing model. In practice these compose: a common LLM family pipeline prunes a big model and then uses distillation to recover the lost accuracy.
fj: Distillation is just training a small model on the same data.
fs: The student matches the teacher's soft logit distribution, not hard labels — and it changes the architecture, unlike quantization or pruning.

followups.q: Why does the temperature T in the distillation loss help the student?
followups.a: A high `T` softens the teacher's softmax, amplifying the small probabilities on wrong classes — the 'dark knowledge' that says, e.g., a dog is more cat-like than truck-like. At `T=1` those signals are nearly zero and the student only sees the hard answer. The `T*T` factor rescales the gradient so the soft-target term keeps the right magnitude.

followups.q: What's the difference between response, feature, and on-policy distillation?
followups.a: Response distillation matches only the teacher's final output logits. Feature distillation also matches intermediate hidden states — richer signal, but needs architecture alignment. On-policy (sequence-level) distillation has the student generate, then scores those samples against the teacher, fixing exposure bias — the modern default for chat models.

followups.q: When does distillation fail to recover the teacher's quality?
followups.a: When the student lacks the capacity to represent the teacher's function — too few parameters to fit a much larger model's distribution — you hit a hard ceiling no amount of soft labels fixes. It also fails if the distillation data doesn't cover the teacher's competent regions, so the student never sees the behaviors you care about. Capacity gap and data coverage are the two limits.

q: What is speculative decoding, why is it lossless, and when does it actually speed things up?
a: A small fast draft model proposes several future tokens, then the large target model verifies them all in a single parallel forward pass; accepted tokens are kept and the first rejected one is resampled from the target's own distribution. Because verification uses the target's distribution to accept/reject, the output is mathematically identical to plain target-model sampling — it is a latency optimization, not a quality trade-off. The speedup depends on the acceptance rate alpha: at alpha ~0.7-0.85 (typical for code or structured chat with EAGLE-3-style drafters) you get roughly 2-3x, but below ~0.5 verification overhead can make it slower than vanilla decoding. Self-drafting variants like Medusa or EAGLE add extra heads to the target model instead of a separate draft model.
fj: Speculative decoding uses a smaller model so the answers are a bit lower quality but faster.
fs: Draft proposes, target verifies in one pass — output is distribution-identical (lossless); speedup tracks acceptance rate, useless below ~0.5.

followups.q: Mechanically, how does verification stay exactly equal to plain target sampling?
followups.a: The target runs one parallel pass scoring all drafted tokens, then accepts each with probability `min(1, p_target/p_draft)`. On the first rejection it resamples from a corrected residual distribution. This modified-rejection-sampling math provably yields the same distribution as sampling from the target alone — lossless regardless of how bad the draft is.

followups.q: Why does speculative decoding help latency but not raw throughput at high batch sizes?
followups.a: It trades extra compute (the wasted draft tokens and parallel verification) for fewer sequential steps, which wins when the GPU is underutilized — low batch, latency-bound single-stream decode. At large batch sizes the GPU is already saturated and compute-bound, so the speculative overhead competes for the same FLOPs and the win shrinks or disappears.

followups.q: Why do EAGLE/Medusa-style self-drafting often beat a separate draft model?
followups.a: A separate draft model is a second model to load, serve, and keep distributionally aligned with the target — low alignment means low acceptance. EAGLE/Medusa add lightweight prediction heads onto the target itself, reusing its hidden states, so the draft is naturally aligned (higher acceptance) and there's no second model in VRAM. EAGLE-3 pushes acceptance high enough for ~3x on code/chat.

q: What tokenization edge cases bite LLM applications, and how do you defend against them?
a: BPE tokenizers split text in non-obvious ways that cause real bugs: multi-digit numbers fragment inconsistently so arithmetic and exact-number matching suffer; whitespace and indentation can eat ~24% of tokens in code, inflating cost; and a trailing space before generation can shift which token the model expects, hurting completions. The nastiest are glitch tokens like 'SolidGoldMagikarp' — strings that earned a vocabulary slot but were nearly absent from model training, so their embeddings are near-random and produce garbled or hallucinated output; studies find ~4% of vocab entries are such under-trained tokens. Defend by tokenizing with the exact model tokenizer (never assume word counts), trimming trailing whitespace, budgeting tokens for code formatting, and validating user-supplied text that may contain adversarial rare tokens.
fj: Tokenization just splits text into pieces; it doesn't really cause bugs.
fs: Calls out digit fragmentation, trailing-space drift, and glitch/under-trained tokens like SolidGoldMagikarp with near-random embeddings.

followups.q: Why exactly does inconsistent digit tokenization hurt arithmetic?
followups.a: If `1234` tokenizes as `12`+`34` in one place and `123`+`4` elsewhere, the model sees different token sequences for related numbers and can't reliably align place values across operands. Newer tokenizers force single-digit (or fixed 3-digit) number splitting precisely to make arithmetic regular. For exact-number matching, normalize numbers or pull them out with deterministic code.

followups.q: How would you actually detect glitch tokens in a vocabulary?
followups.a: Look for tokens whose input embedding is an outlier — far from the cloud of normal embeddings, or with anomalously small norm — which flags under-trained slots. Empirically you probe: feed the token and ask the model to repeat it; glitch tokens produce garbled or evasive output. Then blocklist or normalize any user input containing them before it reaches the model.

followups.q: Why does whitespace-heavy code inflate token cost so much?
followups.a: BPE merges are learned from natural-language frequencies, so runs of indentation spaces and newlines often don't merge into efficient single tokens — each indent level can cost several tokens per line. Across a large file that's the ~24% overhead. Mitigations: send minified or dedented code where structure allows, and always budget tokens using the real tokenizer, not character counts.

q: How does byte-pair encoding (BPE) actually build an LLM's vocabulary?
a: BPE is trained on a corpus before the model: it starts from a base alphabet (raw bytes in modern byte-level BPE) and repeatedly merges the most frequent adjacent symbol pair into a new token, recording each merge as an ordered rule. Training stops at a target vocabulary size, commonly 32K-256K (Llama 3 ~128K, Gemma ~256K). At inference the tokenizer applies those merge rules in the exact learned order, so common words become one token while rare words fragment into subwords. Byte-level BPE guarantees any string is encodable with no unknown-token problem, which is why it handles emoji, code, and any language.
fj: BPE just splits text on spaces and punctuation.
fs: BPE learns merge rules by greedily combining the most frequent byte/char pairs up to a target vocab size, then applies them in order — frequent words become one token, rare words fragment.

code.label: merge most frequent pair
lines: # count adjacent pairs, merge top one

followups.q: Why does byte-level BPE eliminate the unknown-token problem?
followups.a: It starts from the 256 raw bytes as its base alphabet, and any string — any language, emoji, control char — is just a sequence of bytes. So even text the tokenizer has never seen as a merged token always falls back to encodable byte tokens. There's literally no input it can't represent, which is why modern tokenizers handle code, Unicode, and unseen languages without `<unk>`.

followups.q: How does BPE differ from WordPiece and Unigram tokenizers?
followups.a: BPE merges the most frequent pair greedily. WordPiece (BERT) merges the pair that most increases corpus likelihood, a slightly different criterion. Unigram (SentencePiece, used by T5/Gemma) goes top-down: starts with a big vocab and prunes tokens that least hurt likelihood, and can score multiple segmentations probabilistically. Most current LLMs use byte-level BPE.

followups.q: Why does vocabulary size trade off against sequence length?
followups.a: A larger vocab merges more text into single tokens, so sequences are shorter (cheaper, fits more in context) — but the embedding and output softmax matrices grow with vocab size, adding parameters and memory. Too small a vocab fragments everything, inflating sequence length and cost. Picking ~32K-256K balances embedding-table size against tokens-per-word efficiency.

q: Explain top-k, top-p (nucleus), and min-p sampling — how do they differ and which do you reach for?
a: All three truncate the candidate token set before sampling, differing in how the cutoff is chosen. Top-k keeps a fixed k highest-probability tokens, which is rigid: it admits junk when the distribution is flat and over-prunes when it is peaked. Top-p (nucleus) keeps the smallest set whose cumulative probability exceeds p, adapting to the distribution's shape and is the common API default (~0.9-0.95). Min-p sets the threshold relative to the top token's probability (keep tokens above min_p × p_max), so it scales with the model's own confidence and stays coherent even at high temperature, which is why it is favored for creative open-source serving. Sampling truncation composes with temperature; reasoning models are usually best left at their locked defaults.
fj: top-k and top-p are basically the same knob.
fs: Top-k is a fixed count, top-p is a cumulative-probability cutoff that adapts to shape, min-p is a confidence-relative threshold — top-p for APIs, temp+min-p for creative open-source.

followups.q: In what order are temperature and top-p/top-k applied, and does it matter?
followups.a: Conventionally temperature scales the logits first (reshaping the distribution), then top-k/top-p truncate the candidate set, then you renormalize and sample. Order matters: applying temperature after truncation would change which tokens cleared the cutoff. Knowing the engine's order matters when you're combining knobs, because a high temperature widens the nucleus that top-p then keeps.

followups.q: Why does min-p stay coherent at high temperature when top-p doesn't?
followups.a: High temperature flattens the distribution, so top-p's cumulative cutoff sweeps in many junk tokens, producing incoherence. Min-p's threshold is relative to the top token's probability: when the model is confident it stays tight, and only loosens when the model is genuinely uncertain. That confidence-scaling is why creative serving pairs high temp with min-p.

followups.q: Why are reasoning models usually best left at their locked sampling defaults?
followups.a: Reasoning models are RL-trained with a specific sampling regime, and their long chains-of-thought are sensitive to perturbation — aggressive truncation or high temperature can derail multi-step derivations or cause loops. Providers often fix temperature (e.g. 1.0) and discourage custom top-p for these models because off-distribution sampling degrades the reasoning they were tuned to produce.

q: What is few-shot / in-context learning, and when do examples in the prompt actually help?
a: In-context learning is the model inferring a task from input-output examples placed in the prompt at inference time, with no weight updates — zero-shot uses none, few-shot typically 3-8. The examples mostly teach format, output structure, tone, and edge-case handling rather than new knowledge, so they shine for consistent structured output and label conventions. Use representative, diverse, correctly-labeled examples and watch the token cost they add on every call. Notably, on strong recent models few-shot exemplars often add little accuracy over zero-shot and serve mainly to lock the output format, so prefer a tight instruction plus one or two format examples over a long example list.
fj: More examples in the prompt always make it smarter.
fs: Few-shot examples mainly teach format/conventions, not new facts — on strong models they often just align output shape, and each one costs tokens every call.

followups.q: Why can the label distribution and ordering of few-shot examples bias results?
followups.a: Research shows in-context learning is sensitive to surface statistics: if your examples are mostly one label, the model skews toward predicting it (majority-label bias), and recency means the last example exerts extra pull. Mitigate by balancing label counts, shuffling order, and — for classification — calibrating against a content-free input to subtract the prior bias.

followups.q: When does few-shot stop helping or even hurt on strong recent models?
followups.a: On capable instruction-tuned models, a tight zero-shot instruction often matches or beats a long example list, so extra exemplars mostly just burn tokens and latency. They can hurt if examples are inconsistent, unrepresentative, or so long they trigger lost-in-the-middle. Keep one or two examples purely to pin output format, and lean on clear instructions for the task.

followups.q: How does few-shot differ from fine-tuning, since both 'show examples'?
followups.a: Few-shot puts examples in the prompt at inference — no weight changes, instant to iterate, but it costs tokens every call and is capped by context length. Fine-tuning bakes the pattern into weights once, so inference is cheap and you can use far more examples, at the cost of a training run and redeploy. Use few-shot to prototype, fine-tune when the pattern is stable and high-volume.

q: Give the intuition for self-attention: what are query, key, and value, and why three projections?
a: Each token is linearly projected into a query (what I am looking for), a key (what I offer), and a value (the content I contribute). Attention scores come from the dot product of one token's query with every token's key, scaled by 1/sqrt(d_k) to keep softmax gradients stable, then softmaxed into weights that mix the value vectors. Three separate projections matter because relevance is not raw embedding similarity — a pronoun's query can match a noun's key even though the embeddings differ. Multi-head attention runs this in parallel subspaces so different heads capture different relationships (syntax, coreference, position), and causal masking hides future tokens during generation.
fj: Attention just measures how similar two word embeddings are.
fs: Q/K/V are separate learned projections so a token can attend to what is relevant, not merely similar; scores are scaled dot-products softmaxed over values, run per head.

code.label: scaled dot-product attention
lines: if mask is not None: s += mask

followups.q: Why divide scores by sqrt(d_k) before the softmax?
followups.a: Dot products of two `d_k`-dimensional vectors have variance proportional to `d_k`, so without scaling the scores grow large as dimension increases, pushing softmax into saturated regions where one token gets ~all the weight and gradients vanish. Dividing by `sqrt(d_k)` normalizes the variance back to ~1, keeping the softmax in a well-conditioned range so training stays stable.

followups.q: Why use multiple heads instead of one big attention?
followups.a: A single softmax attention can only form one weighted average per token, collapsing all relationships into one pattern. Splitting into heads lets each attend in a different lower-dimensional subspace simultaneously — one head tracks syntax, another coreference, another positional structure. The concatenated heads give the model several distinct relational views per layer for the same compute.

followups.q: What does the causal mask actually do, and why is it needed?
followups.a: It adds `-inf` to score positions for future tokens before the softmax, driving their attention weight to zero so each position attends only to itself and earlier tokens. This enforces autoregressive generation — a token's representation can't peek at tokens it's supposed to predict — and lets the whole sequence be trained in parallel while preserving the left-to-right dependency.

q: What are reasoning models (o3, DeepSeek-R1), and how does test-time compute differ from a bigger base model?
a: Reasoning models are RL-trained to emit a long internal chain-of-thought before the answer, spending extra inference tokens to plan, verify, and self-correct rather than answering in one shot. This is test-time scaling: accuracy rises with thinking budget, so a model can solve problems a larger non-reasoning model misses by thinking longer instead of being bigger. DeepSeek-R1 showed this reasoning behavior can emerge from pure RL with no supervised CoT bootstrapping, matching o1-class performance openly. The trade-offs are real: hidden reasoning tokens cost money and add latency, so route only genuinely hard math, coding, and multi-step tasks to them and keep simple extraction or chat on fast non-reasoning models.
fj: Reasoning models are just bigger models that are smarter.
fs: They are RL-trained to think in tokens before answering, trading inference compute for accuracy — test-time scaling, not more parameters; you pay in latency and hidden reasoning tokens.

followups.q: When is routing a task to a reasoning model the wrong choice?
followups.a: For simple extraction, classification, formatting, or short chat, the long internal chain-of-thought adds latency and hidden-token cost with no accuracy gain — sometimes worse, by overthinking a trivial answer. Reasoning models pay off only on genuinely hard, multi-step math, coding, and planning. Keep the fast non-reasoning model on the easy bulk and route only the hard tail up.

followups.q: How do you control or budget the 'thinking' on a reasoning model?
followups.a: Most expose a reasoning-effort or thinking-token budget (low/medium/high or an explicit max). You tune it per task: higher budget for hard problems where accuracy scales with thinking, lower to cap latency and cost on easier ones. Treat the budget as a quality-vs-cost knob you set from eval data, not a fixed default.

followups.q: Why was DeepSeek-R1 significant for how reasoning ability emerges?
followups.a: It showed strong reasoning can arise from pure RL with verifiable rewards — no large supervised chain-of-thought dataset to bootstrap from — reaching o1-class performance and doing it openly. That matters because it suggests reasoning is an emergent behavior you can elicit with the right reward signal, lowering the data barrier and making the technique reproducible outside frontier labs.

q: What is the difference between an encoder embedding model and a decoder generative model architecturally?
a: Generative LLMs are decoder-only transformers with causal masking: each position attends only to prior tokens and the model is trained to predict the next token, so it produces text autoregressively. Embedding models are typically encoder-style with bidirectional attention (each token sees the whole input) and are trained with contrastive objectives to pool the sequence into one fixed-length vector that places similar texts nearby. That is why you cannot read off a usable sentence embedding from a generative model's hidden states for free — different architecture, masking, and training objective. They are also different deployments: embedding models are smaller, cheaper, and called separately from the generation model in a RAG pipeline.
fj: You can just grab the LLM's hidden state to get an embedding.
fs: Embedding models are bidirectional encoders trained contrastively to pool one vector; generative LLMs are causal decoders trained for next-token prediction — different masking, objective, and deployment.

followups.q: Why does a contrastive objective produce better embeddings than next-token prediction?
followups.a: Contrastive training explicitly pulls representations of similar texts together and pushes dissimilar ones apart, directly shaping a geometry where cosine distance means semantic distance. Next-token prediction optimizes for local continuation, not whole-sequence similarity, so its hidden states aren't organized for retrieval — nearby vectors don't reliably mean nearby meaning.

followups.q: But decoder LLMs are now turned into embedding models — how?
followups.a: Methods like LLM2Vec drop the causal mask to make attention bidirectional, then add a contrastive fine-tuning stage with mean-pooling over tokens. So you can convert a decoder into a strong embedder, but it's a deliberate retrofit — changing the masking and adding a contrastive objective — not just reading off raw generation hidden states.

followups.q: How does pooling choice affect the final embedding quality?
followups.a: You collapse per-token states into one vector via mean pooling, a special token (CLS/EOS), or attention/last-token pooling. The choice interacts with training: a model trained with CLS pooling degrades if you mean-pool at inference. Mean pooling is robust and common for encoders; decoder-derived embedders often use last-token pooling since causal models concentrate sequence info at the end.

q: What are LoRA, QLoRA, and PEFT, and when do you use them over full fine-tuning?
a: PEFT (parameter-efficient fine-tuning) trains a small set of new parameters while freezing the base weights, cutting memory and cost while retaining most full-fine-tune quality. LoRA injects trainable low-rank matrices (a rank-r update B·A) into attention/MLP projections, training often well under 1% of parameters; after training the adapter can be merged into the base for zero added inference latency. QLoRA goes further by loading the frozen base in 4-bit (NF4) and training LoRA adapters on top, letting you fine-tune a 7B-70B model on a single consumer or commodity GPU. Reach for LoRA/QLoRA when you need behavior, format, or domain-style adaptation cheaply and want to serve many task-specific adapters over one base; reserve full fine-tuning for large, fundamental capability shifts.
fj: LoRA fine-tunes the whole model, just faster.
fs: LoRA trains small low-rank adapters on frozen weights (<1% params); QLoRA adds a 4-bit base so big models fit one GPU — and merged LoRA adds no inference latency.

followups.q: How do you choose the LoRA rank r, and what does too-low or too-high cost you?
followups.a: `r` sets the capacity of the `B*A` update. Too low and the adapter can't capture the behavior shift, underfitting. Too high wastes memory and can overfit small datasets, losing the efficiency point. Start around `r=8-16` for style/format adaptation, raise it for harder, more knowledge-heavy tasks, and sweep it on a validation set — `lora_alpha` scales the update so tune them together.

followups.q: If QLoRA trains on a 4-bit base, why isn't the result low quality?
followups.a: The 4-bit (NF4) quantization applies only to the frozen base weights, and gradients/optimizer states stay in higher precision through the trainable adapters. QLoRA adds double-quantization and paged optimizers to manage memory. The base's slight quantization noise is consistent and the adapter learns on top of it, so results land very close to full-precision LoRA at a fraction of the VRAM.

followups.q: How does serving many LoRA adapters over one base model work?
followups.a: Because adapters are tiny and the base is shared, servers like vLLM (with S-LoRA/multi-LoRA) keep one base in VRAM and hot-swap or batch many adapters, routing each request to its adapter. That gives you per-customer or per-task models cheaply. The catch: merging an adapter for zero latency is per-adapter, so dynamic multi-adapter serving keeps them unmerged and pays a small compute overhead.

q: What is the ReAct pattern, and how does it structure an LLM agent loop?
a: ReAct interleaves reasoning and acting: the model emits a thought, chooses a tool action with arguments, your code executes it and feeds back the observation, and the loop repeats until the model produces a final answer. The visible thought-action-observation trace makes the agent steerable and debuggable, and grounding each step in real tool results curbs hallucination compared to reasoning in a vacuum. In production you bound the loop with a max-step limit, validate every tool call against its schema, and handle tool errors as observations the model can recover from. Modern tool-calling APIs implement this natively, so you rarely hand-write the parser, but you still own the executor, retries, and termination logic.
fj: The agent just keeps calling the model until it works.
fs: ReAct is a thought -> action -> observation loop where the model reasons, picks a tool, and you feed the real result back — bounded by max steps, schema validation, and error handling.

followups.q: Why bound the loop with MAX_STEPS — what failure does it prevent?
followups.a: Without a cap, an agent can loop forever: calling the same tool, misreading observations, or oscillating between two actions, burning tokens and money with no progress. `MAX_STEPS` is a hard circuit breaker. When hit, you return a graceful failure or escalate rather than letting a runaway agent rack up cost — essential for any production deployment.

followups.q: How should the agent recover when a tool call errors out?
followups.a: Catch the error and feed it back as the observation — e.g. 'Error: city not found' — so the model can read it and adjust on the next step (retry with corrected args, pick a different tool, or ask the user). Crashing the loop on the first tool failure is brittle; treating errors as recoverable observations is what makes ReAct robust to messy real-world tools.

followups.q: Why does grounding each step in real tool output reduce hallucination?
followups.a: Pure chain-of-thought reasons in a vacuum and can confidently invent facts. ReAct forces the model to fetch real data (search, DB, calculator) and reason over the actual returned observation, anchoring each step to ground truth. The model is checking its reasoning against external reality at every turn, which catches and corrects fabrications mid-loop.

q: RLHF vs DPO — how does each align a model, and why has DPO become popular?
a: Classic RLHF is a two-stage pipeline: train a separate reward model on human preference pairs, then optimize the policy against it with an RL algorithm (PPO) plus a KL penalty to the reference model. It works but is heavy and unstable — reward hacking, mode collapse, and sensitive tuning. DPO derives a closed-form relationship between the optimal policy and the reward, collapsing the two stages into one supervised-style loss directly on preferred/rejected pairs, with no reward model and no sampling during training. That makes DPO far cheaper, more stable, and easier to reproduce, which is why many open models ship with it. RLHF/PPO still wins where you need an explicit reusable reward model or online exploration, and reasoning models lean on RL with verifiable rewards rather than preference data.
fj: DPO is just RLHF with a different optimizer.
fs: RLHF trains a reward model then does PPO; DPO uses a closed form to optimize directly on preference pairs — no reward model, no sampling, more stable and cheaper.

followups.q: What does the KL penalty to the reference model prevent in both methods?
followups.a: It keeps the tuned policy from drifting too far from the original model. Without it, RLHF/PPO will reward-hack — exploiting quirks of the reward model to produce gibberish that scores high but is useless. DPO bakes the same reference constraint into its loss via the `beta` term. The penalty preserves the base model's general capability while nudging behavior toward preferences.

followups.q: When is RLHF/PPO still worth its extra complexity over DPO?
followups.a: When you want an explicit, reusable reward model (to score new outputs or drive best-of-n sampling), or need online exploration where the policy generates fresh samples scored during training. PPO's on-policy data can beat DPO's fixed offline pairs on hard alignment. DPO wins on cost and stability; PPO wins when you need the reward signal as a first-class artifact.

followups.q: Why do reasoning models lean on RL with verifiable rewards instead of preference data?
followups.a: For math and code, correctness is checkable — run the unit tests, verify the answer — so you get a clean, cheap, non-gameable reward signal without collecting human preference pairs. RLVR uses that automatic reward to reinforce reasoning chains that reach correct answers. Preference data is for subjective qualities like helpfulness and tone; verifiable rewards are for objectively-correct domains.

q: What is continuous (in-flight) batching, and why does it beat static batching for LLM serving?
a: Static batching groups requests, runs them together, and cannot release the batch until the slowest sequence finishes, so short generations idle GPU while waiting for long ones. Continuous batching schedules at the token-step granularity: as soon as one sequence emits its end token it leaves the batch and a queued request takes its slot in the next forward pass, keeping the GPU saturated. Combined with PagedAttention managing the KV cache in non-contiguous blocks (like OS paging) to cut fragmentation, this lifts utilization from ~30-40% to well above 80% and multiplies throughput. It is the default in vLLM, TGI, and TensorRT-LLM; it raises aggregate throughput, not single-request latency, and is the main reason self-hosted serving is cost-competitive.
fj: Continuous batching just uses a bigger batch size.
fs: It swaps finished sequences out and queued ones in at each token step instead of waiting for the slowest — GPU stays saturated, throughput multiplies; latency per request is unchanged.
